en.extract:
The Baudó guan (Penelope ortoni) is a species of bird from the family Cracidae. It is restricted to humid forests in the west Andean foothills of western Colombia and north-western Ecuador. It is highly sensitive to hunting and habitat destruction, with large sections of the main distribution in the Chocó having already disappeared. Consequently, it is considered to be endangered by BirdLife International and IUCN.


Distribution and population
Found in Colombia and in Ecuador, on the lower slopes and at the base of the west Andean foothills. There are no confirmed recent records of its presence anywhere south of Pichincha in Ecuador. Its living range and population size has certainly decreased significantly recently. In 2002 the population at one site in Ecuador was estimated to be between 2500 and 7500 adult individuals, with extrapolations based on widespread transects between 1997 and 2006 leading to an extrapolated global population of 7000–21000 mature individuals.


Description
Structurally typical for a guan, the bird is about 65 cm (26 in) tall and the body is mostly dark brown. On the breast and neck there are regular, small white patches. Around the neck and head the brown is slightly darker and tending to greyish-brown, making the patching more visible. The head is plain, a feature that distinguishes this species from all other species of Guan living in that region of South America: all other guans of the same genus have pale markings on the head. Both the legs and the dewlap are red, but the dewlap is brighter and more visible than the legs.

Territorial calls are made around dawn, a far-carrying guttural bawling waou. The birds warn each other of the approach of human with low, soft, and prolonged rising whistles. The alarm call of konh-konh-konh-konh, is repeated several time, but is rarely heard. This alarm call is also shared by other Penelope species.


Diet
In the upper strata it forages for fruits in humid forests, in smaller groups.


Classification
Its closest living relatives are the Band-tailed Guan (Penelope argyrotis), Cauca Guan (Penelope perspicax), Dusky-legged Guan (Penelope obscura), White-crested Guan (Penelope pileata) and the White-browed Guan (Penelope jacucaca). All of these species can also be found in South America, ranging between Columbia and Brazil.
It was previously considered a subspecies of the Andean Guan, but discovery of overlapping populations in western Colombia and southwest Ecuador with no evidence for introgression has led to specific treatment more recently.
The scientific name commemorates the United States naturalist James Orton.


Conservation
In Ecuador the species is protected by law.
Many areas inhabited by the species are poorly known so a first priority for conservation has been implementing population monitoring programs and surveying areas of its habitat. This allows the identification of regions which are poorly protected at the moment and may help extend protected areas in Nariño and Esmeraldas.


References

The '''Baudó guan''' ('''''Penelope ortoni''''') is a species of bird from the family [[Cracidae]]. It is restricted to humid forests in the west [[Andean]] foothills of western [[Colombia]] and north-western [[Ecuador]]. It is highly sensitive to [[hunting]] and [[habitat destruction]], with large sections of the main distribution in the [[El Chocó|Chocó]] having already disappeared. Consequently, it is considered to be [[endangered]] by [[BirdLife International]] and [[IUCN]].<ref name=iucn/>

==Distribution and population==
Found in [[Colombia]] and in [[Ecuador]], on the lower slopes and at the base of the west Andean foothills.<ref>{{cite journal|last1=Vaurie|first1=C|title=Taxonomy of the Cracidae (Aves).|journal=Bulletin of the American Museum of Natural History|date=1968|volume=138|pages=131–260}}</ref> There are no confirmed recent records of its presence anywhere south of [[Pichincha Province|Pichincha]] in Ecuador.<ref name=iucn/> Its living range and population size has certainly decreased significantly recently. In 2002 the population at one site in Ecuador was estimated to be between 2500 and 7500 adult individuals, with extrapolations based on widespread transects between 1997 and 2006 leading to an extrapolated global population of 7000–21000 mature individuals.<ref name=iucn/>

==Description==
Structurally typical for a guan, the bird is about {{cvt|65|cm}} tall and the body is mostly dark brown. On the breast and neck there are regular, small white patches. Around the neck and head the brown is slightly darker and tending to greyish-brown, making the patching more visible. The head is plain, a feature that distinguishes this species from all other species of Guan living in that region of South America: all other guans of the same genus have pale markings on the head. Both the legs and the dewlap are red, but the dewlap is brighter and more visible than the legs.<ref>{{cite journal|title=Handbook of the Birds of the World Alive|url=http://www.hbw.com/species/baudo-guan-penelope-ortoni|website=Handbook of the Birds of the World Alive|doi=10.2173/bow.baugua1.01 |access-date=14 April 2017}}</ref>
[[File:Orton's Guan (Penelope ortoni).jpg|left|thumb|250x250px|Baudó guan (as Orton's guan) appears in a 1908 publication of ''The World's Birds'' by Frank Finn.]]
Territorial calls are made around dawn, a far-carrying guttural bawling ''{{not a typo|waou}}''.<ref>{{cite book|last1=Brooks|first1=D.M.|title=Conserving cracids: the most threatened family of birds in the Americas|date=2006|publisher=ouston Mus. Nat. Sci|isbn=0-9668278-2-1|pages=46–49}}</ref> The birds warn each other of the approach of human with low, soft, and prolonged rising whistles. The alarm call of ''{{not a typo|konh-konh-konh-konh}}'', is repeated several time, but is rarely heard. This alarm call is also shared by other ''Penelope'' species.

==Diet==
In the upper strata it forages for fruits in humid forests, in smaller groups.<ref>{{cite web|title=Penelope ortoni|url=https://neotropical.birds.cornell.edu/portal/species/overview?p_p_spp=77351|website=The Cornell Lab of Ornithology Neotropical Birds|access-date=14 April 2017}}</ref>

==Classification==
Its closest living relatives are the [[Band-tailed Guan]] (''Penelope argyrotis''), [[Cauca Guan]] (''Penelope perspicax''), [[Dusky-legged Guan]] (''Penelope obscura''), [[White-crested Guan]] (''Penelope pileata'') and the [[White-browed Guan]] (''Penelope jacucaca''). All of these species can also be found in South America, ranging between Columbia and Brazil.<ref>{{cite journal|author=Bernis, F|author2=Juana, E|author3=Del Hoyo, J|author4=Fernandez-Cruz, M|author5=Ferrer, X|author6=Saez-Royuela, R|author7=Sargatal, J|title=Nombres en castellano de las aves del mundo recomendados por la Sociedad Española de Ornitología (Segunda parte: Falconiformes y Galliformes)|journal=Ardeola. Handbook of the Birds of the World|date=1994|pages=183–191|url=http://www.ardeola.org/files/285.pdf|access-date=6 April 2017}}</ref><ref>{{cite web|title=South American Birds Index|url=http://www.arthurgrosset.com/sabirds/south%20american%20index.html|access-date=6 April 2017}}</ref>
It was previously considered a subspecies of the Andean Guan, but discovery of overlapping populations in western Colombia and southwest Ecuador with no evidence for introgression has led to specific treatment more recently.<ref>{{cite journal|last1=Hosner|first1=P.A.|last2=Braun|first2=E.L.|last3=Kimball|first3=R.T.|title=Rapid and recent diversification of curassows, guans, and chachalacas (Galliformes: Cracidae) out of Mesoamerica: Phylogeny inferred from mitochondrial, intron, and ultraconserved element sequences.|journal=Molecular Phylogenetics and Evolution|date=2016|volume=102|pages=320–330|doi=10.1016/j.ympev.2016.06.006|pmid=27369454 |bibcode=2016MolPE.102..320H |doi-access=free}}</ref>
The scientific name commemorates the United States naturalist [[James Orton]]{{Citation needed|date=April 2017}}.

==Conservation==
In Ecuador the species is protected by law{{Citation needed|date=April 2017}}.
Many areas inhabited by the species are poorly known so a first priority for conservation has been implementing population monitoring programs and surveying areas of its habitat. This allows the identification of regions which are poorly protected at the moment and may help extend protected areas in Nariño and Esmeraldas.<ref>{{cite web|author=BirdLife International|title=Species factsheet: ''Penelope ortoni''|url=http://datazone.birdlife.org/species/factsheet/baudo-guan-penelope-ortoni/text|website=BirdLife International|access-date=27 April 2017}}</ref>
